name: PayPal Payments API
what_it_does: Payment processing API supporting PayPal wallet payments, card payments, Pay Later (BNPL), Venmo, and international payouts, with a sandbox environment for testing.
use_cases: Accepting PayPal wallet and card payments from consumers who prefer PayPal
International payouts to sellers or contractors via the Payouts API
Marketplace payments with split payment support
Pay Later / Buy Now Pay Later checkout option
not_for: Developer-first payment integrations where API elegance matters (Stripe is better)
High-volume programmatic billing where API complexity is a bottleneck
best_when: Your customers strongly prefer PayPal as a payment method, or you need Venmo/Pay Later options and international payouts.
avoid_when: You prioritize clean API design and developer experience — PayPal's API is older and more complex than modern alternatives.

auth.notes: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow: exchange Client ID + Secret for a short-lived Bearer token. Tokens expire after 8-9 hours and must be refreshed. No long-lived API keys — token management is required. Sandbox and production credentials are separate.

pricing.free_tier_limits: No integration cost; sandbox environment is free
pricing.paid_tiers: 2.99% + fixed fee per transaction for standard card payments; PayPal wallet 3.49% + $0.49; micropayments 5% + $0.05; Payouts $0.25 per payout
pricing.notes: Generally higher transaction fees than Stripe for card payments. PayPal wallet rate is higher but covers a large existing user base. No monthly platform fees.

performance.rate_limits: Default 50 calls/minute for most endpoints; contact PayPal for higher limits. Order creation is among the most sensitive endpoints.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: PayPal's error messages are inconsistent — some are detailed with issue/description fields, others return generic codes. Multiple error schema formats exist across API versions. Debug IDs are available but require contacting PayPal support to interpret.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: PayPal-Request-Id header for order creation provides idempotency for 72 hours. Not consistently supported across all endpoints. Payout items do not support idempotency keys.
known_agent_gotchas: OAuth tokens expire every 8-9 hours — agents must implement token refresh logic or use a token cache
Sandbox and production behave differently in subtle ways — test extensively but expect surprises in production
Order capture is a separate API call from order creation — agents must handle the two-step capture flow
PayPal-Request-Id idempotency window is 72 hours — duplicate IDs outside this window create duplicate orders
Webhook events can arrive out of order and duplicate — agents must implement event deduplication
API versioning is inconsistent — some endpoints use /v1/, others /v2/, with overlapping but different capabilities
Rate limits are poorly documented and may vary by account standing — monitor for 429s aggressively

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced. OAuth 2.0 with short-lived tokens is a reasonable security posture but adds operational complexity. No fine-grained API key scoping. PCI-DSS compliance maintained by PayPal. Webhook signature verification available but optional setup is a risk.
